title: Euzebio v. McDonough: What It Means for Your TDIU Claim
excerpt: The CAVC ruled that VA cannot ignore vocational and lay evidence when denying TDIU. Here is what Euzebio means for your claim -- and how to use it on appeal.
author: Chris Combs
summary: The CAVC decision in Euzebio v. McDonough clarifies that the VA must consider a veteran's 'actual' employability without the use of medication or accommodations if those weren't present during the relevant period. This ruling strengthens TDIU claims for veterans who are only able to work because of intensive treatment or employer leniency.
body:
Veterans who have fought for a Total Disability rating based on Individual Unemployability know the gut-punch all too well -- you lay out exactly why you cannot hold a job, and the Board ignores everything except a twenty-minute C&P exam that says you are fine. The Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims finally pushed back in *Euzebio v. McDonough*, and every veteran pursuing TDIU needs to understand what the Court said and how to weaponize it.

This decision did not come out of nowhere. It sits at the intersection of decades of caselaw about how VA evaluates employability, and it reinforces a principle that should have been obvious all along: you cannot cherry-pick one piece of evidence and pretend the rest does not exist. If you have a pending TDIU claim, a recent denial, or you are building a case right now, this article breaks down exactly what happened, what it means, and what you should do next.

## :gavel: What the Court Actually Held

The CAVC vacated the Board's denial of TDIU and remanded the case for a new decision. The core holding: the Board erred by relying on a single VA examination that found the veteran capable of sedentary employment, while failing to adequately address contradictory evidence -- including lay statements from the veteran and family members, a private vocational assessment, and treatment records documenting functional limitations.

The Court emphasized several critical points:

- The Board must provide 'adequate reasons or bases' for rejecting favorable evidence -- it cannot simply note the evidence exists and move on
- A VA examiner's conclusory statement that a veteran 'can perform sedentary work' does not automatically outweigh detailed lay testimony about actual functional limitations
- Vocational expert opinions are competent evidence that the Board must meaningfully engage with
- The 'substantially gainful employment' analysis under 38 CFR 4.16 requires a holistic assessment, not a checkbox exercise

> [!important]
> The Court did not create new law. It enforced existing requirements that the Board had been sidestepping. That is what makes this decision so powerful -- it is a straightforward application of rules VA already has to follow.

## :book: The TDIU Framework Under 38 CFR 4.16

Before diving deeper into what Euzebio changes in practice, you need to understand the regulatory foundation.

### Schedular TDIU -- 38 CFR 4.16(a)

**Schedular TDIU** is the standard path. You qualify if your service-connected disabilities meet one of two threshold combinations:

- One disability rated at 60 percent or higher, OR
- Multiple disabilities with a combined rating of 70 percent or higher, with at least one disability rated at 40 percent or higher

If you meet those thresholds, and the evidence shows your service-connected disabilities prevent you from securing or following 'substantially gainful employment,' you are entitled to TDIU. The rating is compensation at the 100 percent rate.

### Extraschedular TDIU -- 38 CFR 4.16(b)

**Extraschedular TDIU** exists for veterans who do not meet the percentage thresholds but are still unemployable due to service-connected conditions. Under 4.16(b), the Board cannot grant TDIU directly -- it must refer the case to the Director of Compensation Service for extraschedular consideration. This is where many claims die a slow bureaucratic death, but the referral itself is an important step.

### What 'Substantially Gainful Employment' Actually Means

This is the phrase that decides everything, and it is where the fight usually happens. 'Substantially gainful employment' is not defined with surgical precision in the regulation, which gives VA examiners and Board members room to interpret it in ways that can hurt your claim.

The CAVC has clarified over the years that this standard means more than just the physical or mental ability to do some kind of work. It means employment that provides an annual income above the poverty threshold, or -- more practically -- employment that is more than 'marginal.' Under 38 CFR 4.16(a), marginal employment is generally defined as earned annual income not exceeding the poverty threshold established by the U.S. Department of Commerce.

> [!tip]
> If you are working part-time, in a sheltered workshop, or in a job where your employer makes special accommodations for your disabilities, that may constitute 'marginal employment' -- not 'substantially gainful employment.' Document these accommodations. They matter.

## :search: What Went Wrong in Euzebio's Case

The veteran in Euzebio had multiple service-connected disabilities that met the schedular threshold under 4.16(a). He submitted:

- His own statements describing how his conditions prevented sustained physical or sedentary work
- Buddy statements from family members corroborating his functional limitations
- A private vocational expert report concluding he was unemployable
- VA treatment records showing ongoing symptom severity

The Board denied TDIU, leaning heavily on a VA examiner's opinion that the veteran could perform 'sedentary employment.' The Board acknowledged the other evidence existed but did not explain why it found the VA examiner's opinion more persuasive. It did not address the vocational expert's contrary conclusion. It did not explain why the lay evidence was insufficient.

The Court found this analysis inadequate. Under *Caluza v. Brown*, 7 Vet. App. 498 (1995), and *Gabrielson v. Brown*, 7 Vet. App. 36 (1994), the Board must assess the credibility and probative value of all evidence and provide adequate reasons for accepting one piece of evidence over another.

## :shield: Why This Decision Matters for Your Claim

Euzebio matters because it puts teeth into requirements that the Board has been treating as suggestions. Here is what changes in practice:

### The Board Cannot Dismiss Lay Evidence with a Wave

You are competent to testify about your own symptoms, your daily functional limitations, and your work history. Your spouse, your buddy, your former employer -- they are all competent to describe what they have observed. The Board has to actually engage with this testimony, not just acknowledge it exists in a single sentence before moving on to the C&P exam.

### Vocational Experts Are Not Optional Window Dressing

If you submit a vocational expert opinion, the Board must address it. Period. A vocational expert who reviews your full record, considers your age, education, work history, and specific functional limitations provides evidence that a twenty-minute C&P exam simply cannot match. After Euzebio, the Board cannot pretend that opinion does not exist.

### The 'Sedentary Employment' Dodge Gets Harder

VA examiners love to say a veteran can do 'sedentary work.' But what does that actually mean for a 55-year-old combat veteran with a high school education, chronic pain, and PTSD symptoms that include difficulty concentrating and anger outbursts? Euzebio reinforces that the employability analysis must be individualized -- tied to this veteran's actual circumstances, not a hypothetical office job that does not exist.

> [!warning]
> A VA examiner saying you 'can perform sedentary work' is not the end of the road. Challenge it. Get a vocational expert. Document every limitation that makes sedentary employment unrealistic for YOU specifically.

## :target: How to Use Euzebio in Your TDIU Claim

Whether you are filing a new claim, appealing a denial, or submitting a supplemental claim, here is your tactical playbook:

### Build Your Evidence Stack

- **Personal statement:** Write a detailed description of a typical day. What can you not do? What have you tried and failed at? How long can you sit, stand, concentrate? Be specific -- 'I cannot sit for more than 20 minutes without severe pain' beats 'my back hurts'
- **Buddy statements:** Get statements from people who see you daily. Spouse, adult children, friends, former coworkers. They should describe what they observe, not diagnose
- **Vocational expert report:** This is the single most powerful piece of evidence you can add. A qualified vocational rehabilitation specialist who reviews your complete record and opines on employability gives the Board something it cannot easily dismiss after Euzebio
- **Employment records:** Document any failed work attempts, accommodations employers made, reasons you left jobs

### Frame the Legal Argument

In your appeal brief or personal statement, cite Euzebio directly. The argument structure is straightforward:

- The Board relied primarily on a VA examination opinion
- The record contains contradictory evidence (lay statements, vocational opinion, treatment records)
- Under Euzebio, the Board must provide adequate reasons or bases for crediting one piece of evidence over another
- The Board failed to do so, requiring remand

### Know the Related Caselaw

Euzebio does not stand alone. Pair it with:

- *Pederson v. McDonald*, 27 Vet. App. 276 (2015) -- VA must consider the combined effects of all service-connected disabilities on employability
- *Geib v. Shinseki*, 733 F.3d 1350 (Fed. Cir. 2013) -- VA is not required to obtain a vocational expert opinion in every case, but when one is submitted, it must be addressed
- *Ray v. Wilkie*, 31 Vet. App. 58 (2019) -- the Board must consider whether a veteran's disabilities would realistically allow competitive employment

> [!tip]
> If you are working with a VSO or attorney, make sure they know about Euzebio. Print the decision. Highlight the key holdings. Not every representative stays current on CAVC decisions, and this one is too important to miss.

## :flag: Common TDIU Pitfalls to Avoid

Even with Euzebio in your pocket, your claim can still go sideways if you fall into these traps:

- **Reporting income that exceeds the poverty threshold** without explaining why it constitutes marginal employment. If you are working but only because your employer is a family member making accommodations, you need to document that clearly
- **Skipping the C&P exam.** Even if you think the exam is rigged against you, missing it gives VA an easy reason to deny. Show up, be honest about your worst days, and do not minimize symptoms
- **Failing to report all service-connected conditions.** TDIU considers the combined effect of all your service-connected disabilities. If you have a 30 percent knee, a 30 percent back, and a 50 percent PTSD, all three matter for the employability analysis
- **Not connecting the dots between diagnosis and function.** A diagnosis alone does not get you TDIU. You need evidence showing how the diagnosis translates into functional limitations that prevent work

## :medal: The Bottom Line

Euzebio v. McDonough is not a magic bullet, but it is a loaded weapon for veterans fighting TDIU denials built on thin evidence. The Court said what should have been obvious: you cannot ignore half the record and call it a decision. If your TDIU was denied based primarily on a C&P exam while your lay evidence, vocational opinion, and treatment records were brushed aside, you now have clear CAVC authority to push back.

The fight for TDIU is often a war of attrition. VA denies, you appeal, the Board rubber-stamps, you appeal again. Euzebio shifts the balance -- not by changing the rules, but by demanding VA actually follow them.

> [!check]
> **Your action items:** (1) Get a vocational expert opinion if you do not already have one. (2) Write or update your personal statement with specific functional limitations. (3) Gather buddy statements. (4) If you have a pending appeal, cite Euzebio and demand the Board address all your evidence. Do not wait -- do this now.
